title: Results & Discussion
text:
Motivation









Women who work full-time spend a similar amount of time on paid work but more time on unpaid domestic work.
Results





==============================================
                       Dependent variable:    
                   ---------------------------
                            ACT_WORK          
----------------------------------------------
ACT_UNPAID                  -0.924***         
                             (0.041)          
                                              
female1                      -0.066           
                             (0.172)          
                                              
ACT_UNPAID:female1          0.133***          
                             (0.050)          
                                              
Constant                    7.824***          
                             (0.227)          
                                              
==============================================
==============================================
Note:              *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01




When controlling for \(\,C_i\,\) and time spent on unpaid domestic work, there is no statistically significant difference between males and females’ time spent at paid work.
When controlling for \(\,C_i\,\), each additional hour spent on unpaid domestic work is associated with spending

0.92 fewer hours on paid work for males, and
0.79 fewer hours on paid work for females.




Takeaway: There is evidence of societal expectations that women continue handling domestic work despite similar paid work obligations.



Motivation









Women on average earn less per month than men, but still need to consider controlling for other variables.
Results





==============================================
                       Dependent variable:    
                   ---------------------------
                             WAGELM           
----------------------------------------------
ACT_UNPAID                 -149.369***        
                            (39.678)          
                                              
female1                   -1,517.202***       
                            (164.858)         
                                              
ACT_UNPAID:female1         129.321***         
                            (48.368)          
                                              
Constant                  3,297.729***        
                            (217.416)         
                                              
==============================================
==============================================
Note:              *p<0.1; **p<0.05; ***p<0.01




When controlling for \(\,C_i\,\) and time spent on unpaid domestic work, females on average earn $1,517.20 less per month than males.
When controlling for \(\,C_i\,\), each additional hour spent on unpaid domestic work is associated with earning

$149 less per month for males, and
$20 less per month for females.





Takeaway: Same societal expectations are showing, where time spent on unpaid domestic work has a larger negative effect on male earnings than female earnings.

title: Results & Discussion
text:
Discussion
The empirical results confirm our hypothesis that the time trade-off differs by gender. For women, the results indicate a double-burden. Women must balance both spheres of paid and domestic work.
For men, each additional hour of unpaid work results in near displacement of paid work (\(0.92\) hours). In contrast, women only lose (\(0.79\) hours) of paid work for every hour of domestic labor. Women are more likely to squeeze domestic tasks into their existing schedules, without retreating from the labor market. Thus, the results corroborate that women face a higher total labor burden.
For men, domestic work significantly disrupts earnings (\(-\$149\) per hour). For women, the wage impact is less severe (\(-\$20\) per hour). This may suggest that men’s wages are tied to roles demanding overtime or undivided focus, so domestic work penalizes earning potential. Women may self-select into roles that better align with their domestic responsibilities. Their wages are lower to begin with, reflecting domestic trade-offs. So, additional domestic work has a negligible impact on their earnings.
In both models, gender significantly modifies the relationship between domestic work and economic outcomes. The steeper slopes for men indicate their labor force participation is more sensitive to domestic work. Conversely, women’s labor force participation is far less reactive. Women are expected to work the same amount or more, while earning less on average.

title: Results & Discussion
text:
Limitations

Diary data is messy and inconsistent for early time periods (pre-2010s).

We recognize the existence of potential selection bias within our sample. Are our filters too strict or not strict enough?

Household type is constrained to married couples with kids.
Both spouses are college educated.

Limited to male/female binary and heterosexual relationships

title: Results & Discussion
text:
Extensions for Future Work

Include different demographic variables in regressions as explanatory variables:

Race, Education-Level, Religion, etc.

Include alternative time use variables as outcomes:

Exercise, Leisure, Personal Care, etc.

Time series exploration to examine how these relationships have shifted over time

title: MATH 244: Paid vs. Unpaid Labor by Sex
text: Our research uncovers the time trade-off inherent in individual decision-making. Time is a finite resource, so there’s an associated opportunity cost with each decision to work, sleep, exercise, etc. This begs the question of how do individuals allocate their time in their full 24 hour day? We speculate there must be differences across demographic variables, including sex, age, and marital status.

title: MATH 244: Paid vs. Unpaid Labor by Sex
text:
Research Question
How does time spent doing unpaid domestic work influence wages and time spent doing paid work? Does this relationship differ by sex?

title: Data
text:
Dataset: IPUMS Time Use

Collection of time diary data
American Heritage Time Use Study Extract Builder
From 1930 to 2018, harmonized for comparison over time

Filters:

Day: M-F
Household type: married with kids
Kids under 18 in household
Working-age individuals (25 through 54)
College educated
Filter out missing/dirty values (wage, sex, time use)
Year: 2010s

Transformations:

Convert categorical variables to factors
Convert time use variables to hours per day
Combine time spent on child care and domestic work into one “unpaid domestic work” variable







Variable
Description
Type




WAGELM
Monthly employment income
Numerical


SEX
Individual's sex (female=1, male=0)
Binary


YEAR
Year of the diary entry
Numerical


HHID
Household type (1=married with child)
Categorical


UNDER18
Number of children under 18 in the household
Numerical


AGEYNGST
Age of youngest child
Numerical


EDUC
Education (5=college graduate, 6=post college)
Categorical


AGE
Age of participant
Numerical


ACT_WORK
Daily time spent on paid work
Numerical


ACT_UNPAID
Daily time spent on unpaid domestic work
Numerical


ACT_CHCARE
Daily time spent on child care
Numerical


ACT_CIVIC
Daily time spent on civic, voluntary, and religious activities
Numerical


ACT_EDUCA
Daily time spent on education
Numerical


ACT_LEISURE
Daily time spent on free time (leisure)
Numerical


ACT_PCARE
Daily time spent on personal care
Numerical


ACT_TRAVEL
Daily time spent on travel
Numerical

title: Data
text:
EDA

Unpaid Domestic Work by Sex
We can see that over time, the gap between male and female has shrunk. Time spent by females on unpaid domestic work has remained relatively steady, while male time has increased slightly. However, a disparity still remains: even our most current data from 2018 shows that males on average spend 3.10 hours on unpaid domestic work, while females spend 5.26 hours.











Average Daily Time Use by Sex
This visualization breaks down the 24 hours in a day into the proportion of time spent on various activities, averaged across the 2010s. We see that on average, men spend more time on paid work than unpaid domestic work while women spend less time on paid work and more time on unpaid domestic work.











Clustering in PCA Space
This visualization uses hierarchical clustering to find groupings in PCA space.



The first four PCs are defined below. Each PC represents a linear combination of variables that capture the most variation across individuals in this sample (** = most influential).

title: Methodology
text:
To evaluate how time spent doing unpaid domestic work influences wages and time spent doing paid work, we ran two multiple linear regression models with interaction terms to investigate how this relationship differs by sex. The two economic outcome variables of interest are time spent on paid work and wages.
\[
\text{ACT\_WORK} =
\beta_0 +
\beta_1 \cdot \text{ACT\_UNPAID} +
\beta_2 \cdot \text{SEX} +
\beta_3 \cdot \text{ACT\_UNPAID} \times \text{SEX} +
\delta \cdot C_i
\]
\[
\text{WAGELM} =
\beta_0 +
\beta_1 \cdot \text{ACT\_UNPAID} +
\beta_2 \cdot \text{SEX} +
\beta_3 \cdot \text{ACT\_UNPAID} \times \text{SEX} +
\delta \cdot C_i
\]
In these regressions, \(C_i\) represents our control variables: individuals’ full-time employment status and employment status of partner. The interaction term coefficient \(\beta_3\) represents how the relationship between time spent on unpaid domestic work and the outcome variable varies by sex.







Variable
Description
Type




WAGELM
Monthly employment income
Numerical


ACT_WORK
Daily time spent on paid work
Numerical


ACT_UNPAID
Daily time spent on unpaid domestic work
Numerical


SEX
Individual's sex (female=1, male=0)
Binary
